topic: My smart mailbox lock logged fourteen openings on dates I was on flights outside the country and the bin camera shows a man with my exact gait taking out my mail
title: My Smart Mailbox Logged Fourteen Openings on Days I Was on a Plane. The Camera Shows a Man Who Walks Like Me. | True Scary Story
description_intro: My Eufy smart mailbox lock sent me a weekly summary that said the mailbox at the end of my driveway had been opened fourteen times that week. I had been out of the country for eleven of those days. The interior bin camera had recorded each opening. The man in the footage reached in with my hand, wore my gray jacket, and walked away with my gait. The neighbor across the street told me she waved at me on Thursday. I was in Lisbon.
description:
My Eufy smart mailbox lock sent me a weekly summary that said the mailbox at the end of my driveway had been opened fourteen times that week. I had been out of the country for eleven of those days. The interior bin camera had recorded each opening. The man in the footage reached in with my hand, wore my gray jacket, and walked away with my gait. The neighbor across the street told me she waved at me on Thursday. I was in Lisbon.

I cross referenced every opening against my flight log. The dates lined up with travel days going back four months. The man in the footage took specific letters. Utility statements addressed to me. The medical bill from the urgent care visit last fall. The county property tax envelope. He left every piece of mail addressed to my wife in the bin. The lock log timestamps matched the camera timestamps to the second. I asked the neighbor what I had been wearing when she waved. She described the gray Patagonia jacket I had taken with me to Portugal in my carry on.

There is a frame in the most recent clip that I have not shown to my wife. The man finishes taking the mail. He pauses at the open bin. He turns his head. He looks directly into the lens. His face is mine. He is older by perhaps three years. He nods once. Watch to the end. The official cluster box at the end of the street has started logging openings on the city audit system on the dates I am out of town.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: The Eufy app sends me a weekly summary on Sunday nights. The summary covers the smart lock on my mailbox at the end of the driveway. Most weeks it tells me the mailbox was opened two times. Tuesday and Saturday. The two days I check the mail. This week the summary told me the mailbox had been opened fourteen times. Fourteen openings in seven days. I read the number three times before I understood what I was looking at. I had been out of the country for eleven of those seven days. I had landed home on the previous Friday after a long work trip. The summary covered the full week before my return. Eleven of those days I had been in Lisbon and Madrid. The openings were spread across all seven days of the week.

close shot of a kitchen counter with a phone face up and a glass of water beside it, the phone screen glowing, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a printed flight boarding pass on a kitchen counter showing a return date in faded print, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: We live in a quiet cul de sac at the end of a long residential street. Six houses arranged around a small turning circle. Each house has a curbside post mounted USPS mailbox at the foot of the driveway. Ours is the black powder coated kind with the small red flag on the side. The post is four feet tall. The bin sits at chest height. Last spring I installed a Eufy smart lock on the inside of the bin flap. I also installed a small interior camera in the upper corner of the bin. The camera points down at the inside of the mailbox. The lens is the size of a dime. The footage uploads to the same app as the lock. The app stores thirty days of clips on the free shared tier.
image_prompts: wide exterior of a suburban cul de sac at dusk with six modest houses arranged around a small turning circle, no figures, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a black powder coated USPS mailbox on a tall wooden post with a small red flag raised, late afternoon light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a small black smart lock attached to the inside of an open mailbox flap, the bolt visible, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a dime sized camera lens mounted in the upper corner of an empty mailbox interior, dim ambient light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close shot of a phone app screen showing a clip library list with thirty days of recorded entries, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I check the mailbox on Tuesdays and Saturdays. My wife rarely uses it. She prefers paperless billing for everything she handles. I keep a few accounts on paper out of habit. The utilities. The property tax. The medical billing from the urgent care chain my insurance uses. I unlock the bin from the app on my way down the driveway. I take out whatever is there. I lock it again before I walk back inside. The lock log records every open and close. The camera records ten seconds before and ten seconds after each event. I had never had a reason to scroll through the log. The summary at the end of the week had always matched my own behavior. Two events. Two clips. Two timestamps that lined up with my Tuesday and Saturday walk down the driveway.

close shot of a smart lock event log on a phone screen showing two timestamps for one week, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I opened the full lock log inside the app and scrolled back four months. The pattern of two openings a week held for a long time. Then I started seeing extra openings. Single events on weekdays I never check the mail. I pulled out a notebook and wrote down the dates. January fourteenth. February third. February nineteenth. March eighth. There were more. I opened the calendar app on the same phone. I pulled up my flight log from the airline that handles my work travel. January fourteenth had been a return leg from Lisbon. February third had been an outbound to Madrid. February nineteenth had been a same day connection through Lisbon. March eighth had been the second leg of a Lisbon Madrid Lisbon round trip. Every extra opening lined up with a day I had been on a plane.
image_prompts: close shot of a phone screen showing a scrolled smart lock log with multiple timestamps highlighted, dim desk light, horizontal 16:9 framing
close detail of a small notebook page with handwritten dates listed in pencil, the script tight and even, horizontal 16:9 framing

wide exterior of a darkened suburban driveway at night with a mailbox at the far end and a single porch light glowing, no figures, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I closed the app. I sat down at the kitchen counter. I told myself three things. One. The smart lock had a clock chip and the clock chip could drift across months without a manual resync. The timestamps were probably stamped wrong by several hours and what looked like flight days were actually evening events from the day before. Two. The free shared tier of the camera might pull footage from a nearby device with a similar serial number. There had been a recall notice about that on a forum I had read in March. Three. My wife sometimes checked the box if she was expecting a card from her mother. She might have used my app login. None of those three things explained eleven openings in eleven travel days. I knew they did not. I told them to myself anyway.

sections.narration: I opened the camera tab and pulled up the clip from January fourteenth. The interior of the bin came into view. A hand reached down through the flap. The hand was a right hand. The cuff above the wrist was gray. The hand sorted through the small stack of mail. It pulled out three envelopes and left the rest. The clip ended. I opened the next clip. February third. The same hand. Three envelopes taken. The rest left. I went through every extra clip in order. Every extra opening took the same kind of letters. The utility envelope from the gas company. The urgent care medical bill. The county property tax envelope. Every envelope addressed to me. Not a single envelope addressed to my wife had ever been touched. The clips ended with the flap closing softly.

sections.narration: I walked across the cul de sac on Saturday morning. Mrs. Dale lives in the small ranch house directly across from ours. She has lived there for thirty one years. She sits on her front porch most afternoons with a glass of iced tea. I rang the bell and asked if she had noticed anyone unusual at our mailbox during the past few weeks. She laughed. She said no, only you. She said she had waved at me on Thursday afternoon around four and I had waved back. She said I had been wearing my gray Patagonia jacket. I had been in Lisbon on Thursday afternoon. I had eaten lunch at a small restaurant near the cathedral at four local time. The gray Patagonia jacket had been folded in the bottom of my suitcase. The suitcase had been in the hotel closet.

sections.narration: I went home and pulled up the most recent extra clip. The one from Thursday afternoon. I played it once at full speed. I played it again at quarter speed. I played it a third time frame by frame using the scrub bar. The clip began with the flap opening from outside. The hand entered as before. This time the camera caught more than the hand. The man stepped slightly closer to the bin between frames. The angle caught part of his sleeve. The gray Patagonia fleece. The cuff on the left wrist. A wristwatch I have worn for nine years. The scuffed gold wedding band on the ring finger. A faint brown coffee stain on the lower edge of the cuff. I had noticed that same stain on my own cuff the week before I left for Lisbon.
image_prompts: close shot of a laptop screen showing a paused video frame of an arm in a gray fleece reaching into a mailbox, soft pixelation, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I opened the clip recorded earlier today. The man arrived at the box at two oh seven in the afternoon. The flap opened. The same hand reached in. He took three envelopes. The same kind. The utility. The medical bill. The tax envelope. He folded them into the inside pocket of the gray fleece. Then he did not close the flap. He paused. His hand rested on the lip of the bin. He turned his head slowly. He looked directly into the small camera lens in the upper corner of the bin. The lens caught the full front of his face. The face was mine. The features were exact. The age was wrong. He was older by perhaps three years. The hair at the temples was thinner. He looked into the lens for a count of four. Then he nodded once.

sections.narration: I removed the Eufy lock that night. I unscrewed the camera from the upper corner of the bin. I put both devices in a shoebox on a high shelf in the garage. The mailbox sat empty and unlocked for the next two weeks. The lock log went silent. The clip library stopped updating. I told myself it was over. Then the property manager for our subdivision sent me a polite email. The official USPS cluster box at the end of the street, the one the carrier uses for parcel overflow, had started logging openings on the city audit system. The openings were on the four dates I had been out of town that month. The carrier called me on a Tuesday afternoon. She asked if I had requested a second key for the cluster box last week. I had not.
